original_abstract: In nature, enzymatic heme and non-heme high-valent iron-oxo species readily activate strong C–H bonds; however, recreating this potent reactivity within synthetic systems remains a significant challenge. This difficulty stems not only from the inherent instability of the terminal iron-oxo moiety but also from the steric and electronic constraints that frequently hamper the performance of synthetic mononuclear iron(IV)-oxo complexes. Crucially, while biological non-heme iron catalysts utilize high-spin (S = 2) ground states to achieve their remarkable activity, this electronic configuration is notoriously difficult to stabilize in isolated molecular species. We demonstrate that these limitations can be overcome by using metal-organic frameworks to provide site-isolated iron centers within a constrained, weak-field ligand environment. Specifically, the framework Fe2(dobdc) and its magnesium-diluted variant, Fe0.1Mg1.9(dobdc), facilitate the catalytic oxidation of ethane to ethanol and acetaldehyde using nitrous oxide as a terminal oxidant. Electronic structure calculations suggest that this reactivity is driven by a high-spin S = 2 iron(IV)-oxo intermediate, mimicking the strategy employed by nature’s most effective oxidation catalysts.
humanized_abstract: Synthetic enzymatic high-valent iron-oxo species capable of activating very strong C–H bonds are scarce. There are two main obstacles that need to be overcome: i) the instability of the iron-oxo core, and ii) the fact that a large number of iron complexes bearing artificial ligands that can activate very strong C–H bonds do not work well because of steric reasons and poor activation energy barriers. Moreover, while enzymatic non-heme iron(IV)-oxo species have a high-spin ground state with S = 2, which is one of the main contributions to their high reactivity, this electronic state is difficult to stabilize in solution and in isolated molecules. Herein, we show that metal-organic frameworks can be a very powerful approach to prepare isolated catalytic sites that have the desired electronic and steric properties to be active in hydrocarbon oxidation. Our studies demonstrate that the metal-organic framework material Fe2(dobdc) as well as the dilute magnesium analogue Fe0.1Mg1.9(dobdc) catalyze efficiently the oxidation of ethane into ethanol and acetaldehyde using nitrous oxide as the oxidant. Based on experimental and theoretical studies, the catalytic activity is assigned to a high-spin iron(IV)-oxo species with S = 2, similar to the one postulated in the non-heme iron enzymes that are capable of activating very strong C–H bonds.
